case_name: Chaina Ram Versus State of H.P.
court: High Court of Himachal Pradesh, Shimla
facts: On 11.03.2003 at the old bus stand in Palampur a bus bearing registration No. HP53-2713 was being boarded by passengers when Akshay Kumar fell while boarding and his left leg was crushed under the rear tyre. The accused, Chaina Ram, was the driver of the bus. Police investigated, a mechanical inspection found no defect, and medical evidence recorded simple and grievous injuries to Akshay Kumar. A challan was presented and the accused was tried for offences under Sections 279, 337, 338 IPC and Section 181 of the Motor Vehicles Act.
arguments_claimant: The petitioner (accused) argued that the evidence was contradictory and established that Akshay Kumar attempted to board a moving bus and was negligent himself, so there was no negligence on the part of the driver. The petitioner also contended that a driving licence had been produced before the Appellate Court (a point disputed by the State) and alternatively sought leniency on sentence given the long delay since the incident.
arguments_respondent: The State submitted that the accused drove the bus without a valid driving licence, started the bus while passengers were boarding and thereby acted negligently, and therefore the revisions should be dismissed and convictions upheld.
ratio_decidendi: The High Court held that "It is well settled that in the exercise of revisional jurisdiction under Section 482 of the Criminal Procedure Code, the High Court does not, in the absence of perversity, upset concurrent factual findings. It is not for the Revisional Court to re-analyse and re-interpret the evidence on record." The Court applied established principles on the limited scope of revisional jurisdiction (Sections 397/482 CrPC), observing that the revisional court "is not meant to act as an appellate court" and may interfere only where findings are "perverse or wholly unreasonable" or based on no material. On the factual question of negligence, the Court relied on Mohd. Aynuddin to state that "Merely because a passenger fell from the bus while boarding the bus, no presumption of negligence can be drawn against the driver of the bus," and that prosecution must show the driver moved the bus without getting a signal; absent such evidence negligence cannot be fastened. Applying these principles, the Court concluded that the trial and appellate courts erred in treating the passenger's fall itself as proof of negligent driving, but that absence of a produced driving licence properly sustained conviction under Section 181 MV Act.
final_order: The revision petition was partly allowed: the convictions and sentences under Sections 279, 337 and 338 IPC were set aside, whereas the conviction under Section 181 of the Motor Vehicles Act and the fine imposed was upheld. The records of the courts below are to be returned accordingly.
summary: This is a criminal revision by a bus driver (Chaina Ram) against convictions for negligent driving causing injuries to a passenger who fell while boarding. The High Court reaffirmed the narrow scope of revisional jurisdiction and held that it will not reappreciate evidence or upset concurrent findings absent perversity; it also held that a passenger falling while boarding does not automatically create a presumption of driver negligence. The High Court set aside convictions under Sections 279, 337 and 338 IPC but upheld conviction under Section 181 MV Act for driving without producing a valid licence. Practically, the judgment limits revisional interference and reiterates that absence of licence is not by itself proof of rashness but can sustain an offence under Section 181 MV Act if licence is not produced.
embedding_summary: Chaina Ram v. State of H.P., High Court of Himachal Pradesh, Shimla, Cr. Revision No. 59 of 2013; 2025:HHC:25012; Date of Decision 30.7.2025. Facts: bus (Reg. HP53-2713) at Palampur on 11.03.2003, passenger Akshay Kumar fell while boarding and suffered crushing injury to left leg; accused driver charged under Sections 279, 337, 338 IPC and Section 181 Motor Vehicles Act. Key legal issues: scope of revisional jurisdiction under Sections 397/482 CrPC (reliance on Amit Kapoor, Malkeet Singh Gill, Dilipsinh Rao, Kishan Rao, Bir Singh), whether presumption of negligence arises when a passenger falls (relying on Mohd. Aynuddin), and legal effect of not producing a driving licence (State Government v. Bhawanesh Kumar; Suleman Rehiman Mulani). Outcome: revision partly allowed — convictions under 279/337/338 IPC set aside; conviction under Section 181 MV Act for driving without valid licence upheld; no compensation awarded. Significance: affirms narrow revisional powers, requires positive proof that driver moved vehicle without signal to fasten criminal negligence, and confirms that absence of a licence does not automatically equal rashness though it sustains an offence under Section 181 MV Act.
legal_principles: Revisional court under Sections 397/482 CrPC cannot re-appreciate evidence or act as an appellate court and will not upset concurrent factual findings absent perversity
No presumption of negligence when a passenger falls while boarding; prosecution must prove the driver moved the vehicle without signal
Absence of a driving licence does not by itself constitute rashness or negligence
Failure of accused to produce driving licence can sustain conviction under Section 181 Motor Vehicles Act
Revisional interference is warranted only for patent defect, error of jurisdiction or perversity in findings
cases_cited: Malkeet Singh Gill v. State of Chhattisgarh, (2022) 8 SCC 204
Dilipsinh Kishorsinh Rao (State of Gujarat v. Dilipsinh Kishorsinh Rao), (2023) 17 SCC 688
Amit Kapoor v. Ramesh Chander, (2012) 9 SCC 460
Sanjaysinh Ramrao Chavan v. Dattatray Gulabrao Phalke, (2015) 3 SCC 123
Bir Singh v. Mukesh Kumar, (2019) 4 SCC 197
Southern Sales & Services v. Sauermilch Design and Handels GmbH, (2008) 14 SCC 457
Mohd. Aynuddin v. State of A.P., (2000) 7 SCC 72
State Government v. Bhawanesh Kumar, 1957 SCC OnLine MP 102
Suleman Rehiman Mulani v. State of Maharashtra, 1967 SCC OnLine SC 337 : (1968) 2 SCR 515 : AIR 1968 SC 829
bm25_text: Chaina Ram Versus State of H.P. High Court of Himachal Pradesh, Shimla 2025 On 11.03.2003 at the old bus stand in Palampur a bus bearing registration No. HP53-2713 was being boarded by passengers when Akshay Kumar fell while boarding and his left leg was crushed under the rear tyre. The accused, Chaina Ram, was the driver of the bus. Police investigated, a mechanical inspection found no defect, and medical evidence recorded simple and grievous injuries to Akshay Kumar. A challan was presented and the accused was tried for offences under Sections 279, 337, 338 IPC and Section 181 of the Motor Vehicles Act. The petitioner (accused) argued that the evidence was contradictory and established that Akshay Kumar attempted to board a moving bus and was negligent himself, so there was no negligence on the part of the driver. The petitioner also contended that a driving licence had been produced before the Appellate Court (a point disputed by the State) and alternatively sought leniency on sentence given the long delay since the incident. The State submitted that the accused drove the bus without a valid driving licence, started the bus while passengers were boarding and thereby acted negligently, and therefore the revisions should be dismissed and convictions upheld. The High Court held that "It is well settled that in the exercise of revisional jurisdiction under Section 482 of the Criminal Procedure Code, the High Court does not, in the absence of perversity, upset concurrent factual findings. It is not for the Revisional Court to re-analyse and re-interpret the evidence on record." The Court applied established principles on the limited scope of revisional jurisdiction (Sections 397/482 CrPC), observing that the revisional court "is not meant to act as an appellate court" and may interfere only where findings are "perverse or wholly unreasonable" or based on no material. On the factual question of negligence, the Court relied on Mohd. Aynuddin to state that "Merely because a passenger fell from the bus while boarding the bus, no presumption of negligence can be drawn against the driver of the bus," and that prosecution must show the driver moved the bus without getting a signal; absent such evidence negligence cannot be fastened. Applying these principles, the Court concluded that the trial and appellate courts erred in treating the passenger's fall itself as proof of negligent driving, but that absence of a produced driving licence properly sustained conviction under Section 181 MV Act. The revision petition was partly allowed: the convictions and sentences under Sections 279, 337 and 338 IPC were set aside, whereas the conviction under Section 181 of the Motor Vehicles Act and the fine imposed was upheld. The records of the courts below are to be returned accordingly. This is a criminal revision by a bus driver (Chaina Ram) against convictions for negligent driving causing injuries to a passenger who fell while boarding. The High Court reaffirmed the narrow scope of revisional jurisdiction and held that it will not reappreciate evidence or upset concurrent findings absent perversity; it also held that a passenger falling while boarding does not automatically create a presumption of driver negligence. The High Court set aside convictions under Sections 279, 337 and 338 IPC but upheld conviction under Section 181 MV Act for driving without producing a valid licence. Practically, the judgment limits revisional interference and reiterates that absence of licence is not by itself proof of rashness but can sustain an offence under Section 181 MV Act if licence is not produced.
